author: @Skiptickle
text: Bud light has a huge problem.  They are the easiest product in the world to boycott.  Budweiser has a huge share of the beer market.  However, they don't have a particularly good product and there's tons of competition out there.  As soon as a loyal customer boycotts and tries a new beer, chances are they're going to find something that they like better.  So riddle me this Batman, even once they get over being mad at Bud, why would they go back?

author: @andrewtaylor940
text: To put this in perspective, let’s do a little math. Bud Light sells at around 1-3 points of profit margin. Profits are made on the shear volume sold. The first weekend, and weekends are the key days beer sells, Bud Light sales dropped somewhere between 30-50%. Best case is that one weekend wiped out 10 weeks worth of profits. Worst case it’s almost six months. Never let them tell you Boycotts don’t work. Never let them tell you your sales don’t matter. Never let them tell you you are disposable and replaceable as a customer. We are seeing the same thing with media such as Disney. Marvel Movies and Animated movies are only coming in at best with half the numbers they were doing 2 years ago. It’s not “Super Hero Fatigue”. It’s they failed to offer an acceptable product for half of the movie going audience and that half walked away. The people got fed up with being lectured to, told they were evil racist Nazi’s. So they walked away. They got sick of seeing open predation on children in and by media, so they walked away. The power is not in their sales numbers or box office. The power is in their Profit Margin. We are that profit margin. They cannot replace us. And they need us far more than we need them.

replies.author: @davidedwards8732
replies.text: Not just any weekend. It was Easter weekend. If you cannot sell beer when the weather is finally getting over winter and it is a national holiday you should give up. As for the moral blackmail of pointing out potential staff job losses, it is of there own doing and I am sure all the other beer companies suddenly need to hire now. Not the idiot in a dress who really offended me. It was the VP who openly insulted the  customers.

text: Found this post on the predominantly progressive internet forum Datalounge on Wednesday, April 12, under the chat entry entitled "Anheuser-Busch has already lost more than $5 BILLION DOLLARS in value":  "A friend of mine went out over the weekend with someone that [sic] works for the PR firm representing Anheuser-Busch [AB]. My friend filled me in on the details. It's an absolute total s**tshow over at AB right now. As of the weekend, AB's strategy was to issue a statement mid week that was in essence a 'non apology' but more of a 'we understand' type of thing. Part two of their plan was to release a pivot type of ad/campaign in three weeks in hope to divert away from this in hopes that in time, it would die down. They used a 'Colin Kappernic [sic]' type of analogy in one of their pitch meeting[s]..."

author: @OneManShow1964
text: I am not a beer drinker. This whole matter means nothing to me. Companies who provide goods should focus on marketing those goods. Why do they seek to be political? That move is instantly polarizing. Anyone with any sense for business should know that, right?

replies.author: @mortyrosenstein4211
replies.text:
Not anymore. The VP who did this is an Ivy leaguer. Business is no longer about just business. ESG is being taught to these people, it’s supposed to be woven into everything they can. 

It’s not an accident that BL, a beer with a very blue collar image, was targeted for the message. This was very intentional. The goal is to saturate every single inch of American life with the message. If they had pulled this off, with no backlash, this is a HUGE win for them. 

But the problem is, this crossed the line. There is a backlash. Average citizen is now sick of the message and refusing to tolerate it. 

It’s not longer about money. Look at Disney. Look at Hollywood. The message is more important than the product, even at the cost of profit. 

They are all rich. The VP at BL lives in an 7 million dollar home. They don’t care about profit, because it doesn’t affect them. They get paid anyway. The are rewarded for pushing the message, not large profits. 

Times have changed.

author: @scottguitar8168
text: They give the appearance of attempting to smooth things out with their core base, but still want to go woke, which is what brought all of this backlash about. I know there are other people and companies who can't be cancelled by cancel culture and that there are people standing up to cancel culture even having success at cancelling some of them. I think my point here is that I don't think Anheuser Busch fears cancel culture and there are plenty of beer companies not going down this woke road which implies Busch wanted to go down this road of their own free accord. It can't be to pick up new customers since there are more young people coming from conservatives who believe in family, thus have more children as a result. There is certainly a reason Busch wanted to go down this road, but it's none of the reasons that would make sense for a business. They are not after new customers, they are not scared of cancel culture, they knew there would be some backlash from their core customer base, they are not actually apologizing nor firing the woke VP that they purposefully hired to go down this woke road. The go woke businesses lose money and customers every time, from Hollywood and Disney, to liberal media that continues to attempt to spread propaganda and supports all things wrong, including woke culture to the few viewers they have left. If it is not about the money, what is it truly about because they certainly don't care about the people? My guess is that it is ultimately about power as they attempt this Marxist campaign in the West and other countries are attempting to fight back against this cancer to society.

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text: I am not certain this feels like cancel culture. This is a circumstance when people perceive they are being slighted or taken for granted. As you say, this backlash was likely predicted. They are making clear moves into the Neo-Marxist space with ESG and other influences driving them that direction.

author: @girlinvt
text: Mulvaney is a Tik Tok influencer. A platform that has literally 42% of its viewers in the 13-18 range, and the lion share in the 18 to 34 year range. So Anheuser Busch is basically advertising to the under 21 age group. Add in the fact that Mulvaney is 28 years old but acts like someone in their teens, or a girl not grown past 13 in a bid to fit in and be accepted by the viewership. Mulvaney is basically the modern version of Al Jolson, Mulvaney parodying every dismissive stereotype of women ever used to denegate them is insulting.

author: @ralf2528
text: what makes this campaign against budweiser so funny is, there are many more companies surfing the woke wave. so if it would be about all this woke nonsense, shouldn't all these firms be boycotted? why does a commercial make people decide what beer people drink, instead of taste? how can you even demand an apology? is there a law that companies can do only commercials that certain customers approve? this whole thing is ridiculous....what is it that these "beer-rebels" are trying to achieve? obviously not that all woke crap ends, since they buy tons of stuff from other woke companies. what exactly would they demand an apology for? ...blessed are those who are poor in mind, for they know not what they do...

replies.author: @CultureCasino
replies.text:
There are many brands in the world that exceed or rise above their name. The become ubiquitous or synonymous with their need or offer. People take a sense of ownership of it. Kleenex is a brand of facial tissue, yet everyone asks for a Kleenex when they have a box of Puffs... 

In this instance people feel betrayed. They think that their beer brand is part of America. Hence an expectation of apology.
